title: Signals and stigmas from banking interventions: Lessons from the Bank Holiday of 1933
abstract: A nationwide panic forced President Roosevelt to declare a banking holiday in March 1933. The government reopened banks sequentially using a process that sent noisy signals about banks’ health. New microdata reveals that the public responded to these signals. Deposits at rapidly reopened banks rebounded quicker than at comparable or stronger banks that reopened even a few days later. The stigma of late reopening shifted funds from stigmatized to lauded banks and among communities that they served. Despite persisting over a decade, the shift had no measurable impact on the rate at which localities recovered from the Great Depression.

author.Matthew Jaremski: Utah State University and NBER, United States
author.Gary Richardson: University of California, Irvine, and NBER, United States|||Tsinghua University, China

article.1. Introduction: During systemic banking panics, policymakers seeking to stem the stampede must convince investors that their funds are safe (Gorton and Tallman, 2018). This task often involves across-the-board, treat-all-depositors-and-firms equally interventions. Following the failure of Lehman Brothers in 2008, the government rushed to guarantee liabilities at many financial firms in afflicted sectors, even those which had hitherto been ineligible for assistance, such as money market mutual funds. Critics - including Chang and Velasco (2000), Lacker (2008), Farhi and Tirole (2012), Chari and Kehoe (2016), and Keister (2016) - assert that unconditional assistance can be pernicious because expectations of bailouts, particularly those not conditioned on pre-panic behaviors, incentivize risk-taking and engender instability.1 Policymakers, however, argue that panic-ending interventions have to be unconditional to prevent the stigma of assistance from afflicting institutions revealed to be weaker and impeding economic recovery. Leaders of the Federal Reserve, for example, raised the specter of this bank stigma to justify their actions during the Global Financial Crisis [hereafter GFC] (United States Financial Crisis Inquiry, 2011; Geithner, 2014).2Regulators’ fears of “bank stigma,” where banks receiving government assistance lose the confidence of market participants, are distinct from the more widely researched concept of “facility stigma,” where financial institutions become reluctant to seek assistance from government programs that might publicize their need for assistance. The concept of bank stigma justifies policymakers’ sweeping programs to counteract panics, although little empirical evidence underlies their beliefs that signals sent by triaging applicants for aid and targeting assistance to institutions in need through no fault of their own produce substantial, pernicious stigmas. Evidence is limited because potentially stigma inducing interventions during systemic banking crises have not been observed in OECD economies for nearly a century. Systemic crises seldom occur, and policymakers fear stigma to such an extent that rescue packages have been designed to prevent it from arising. Policymakers’ suppositions about stigma, in other words, lead them to implement policies that prevent researchers from testing those suppositions.To shed light on this debate, we analyze the largest and most recent federal intervention during a systemic banking crisis that could have sent signals about the health of banks, the Bank Holiday of March 1933. At the nadir of the Great Depression, the U.S. government ordered all depositories in the nation to cease operations and then over time authorized individual banks to reopen. Officials worried that their plan of action - which sequenced reopenings based upon imperfect information about banks’ health as well as characteristics of their communities - might stigmatize banks because their reopening dates were readily observed and expected to be predicated on their health. Our essay ascertains whether sequential reopening signaled banks’ health, the accuracy of those signals, and the impact of bank stigma on the broader recovery.Characteristics of the Bank Holiday facilitate identification of links between regulatory intervention, bank stigma, and economic activity. First, the Holiday was mandatory and the signal sent by regulators - the date of reopening - was salient to depositors and observed by all market participants. All banks closed on the same date and reopened when regulators told them to reopen, eliminating any selection into treatment or observation of treatment. Second, before the initial waves of reopenings, regulators did not provide financial aid to banks or introduce novel financing facilities. Banks had the same balance sheets as the day they were closed and had access to the same federal lending programs that they had access to before the Holiday began. Depositors, likewise, had the same deposits, and borrowers had the same loans. Third, regulators’ decisions about when to reopen banks were based on public and private information about the health of banks as well as other political, social, and economic goals set by federal and state authorities. For this reason, a bank's reopening date could reveal private information about its health that depositors could not previously observe, but might be misleading because other factors (like political concerns) could also have induced regulators to accelerate or postpone its reopening. Fourth, almost all banks operated within a single county, which allows us to compare stigma's impact on individual banks to outcomes in the communities in which they operated.Aspects of the historical era also pose challenges for our analysis. Data available on banks, for example, is much more detailed today than in the past, when data from banks typically specified a few categories of assets and liabilities and when information concerning bank management quality and loan losses was neither widely nor systematically available. Data about the economy are also more widely available today than in the past. In the 1930s, for example, national income and product accounts had yet to be created, and little data on economic activity at the state and county level existed except that provided by periodic censuses of populations and businesses. The limited information available enhances the credibility of our argument, since stigmas are more likely to arise in limited-information environments, but may limit its external validity, an issue that we will return to in the conclusion. Limited information also complicates our empirical analysis, since some factors that scholars might control for when analyzing modern data - like loan losses, management quality, or local unemployment rates - cannot be considered because the data were not collected in the past. Limited information prevents us from completely ruling out some alternative hypotheses concerning factors that could have influenced the post-Holiday rebound in deposits but which we (as well as regulators and economic agents in the 1930s) do not observe.Our argument proceeds in four steps. 2 Historical background, 3 Data set the stage. Section 2 elaborates on the characteristics of the Holiday and the structure of the banking system that facilitate inference in our analysis. These characteristics coincide with the prerequisites of information revelation in the game-theoretic literature on signaling and stigma.3 Section 3 describes the novel nationwide micro-database that we collect, collate, and digitize to enable our analysis and discusses patterns apparent in the data.The second step of the argument, appearing in Section 4, demonstrates that regulators’ decisions during the Holiday were equivalent to standard game-theoretic models of signals and stigmas, where an informed party (regulators) makes an observable choice (reopening date) based upon private information (bank health). We regress reopening dates on information from bank balance sheets as well as an array of regulatory, demographic, geographic, and political variables. This exercise confirms the essential assumption of the models: the correlation between information held by and the action chosen by the signalers (regulators). We show reopening dates were correlated with bank health. Banks with more resources, larger liquidity reserves, and bigger capital buffers on average reopened sooner than banks with fewer resources, less liquidity, and less capital. This result, therefore, demonstrates that rapid reopening signaled banks’ health to market participants. These regressions, however, also reveal the imperfection of the signal. Many weak banks reopened early, while many strong banks reopened late.4The third step of our argument, appearing in Section 5, explores how stigma influenced depositors’ decisions. This exercise confirms two key predictions of signaling models, that less informed agents (depositors) respond to the information that they receive and that stronger signals generate stronger responses. We identify stigma's impact by comparing changes in deposits between the summers of 1932 and 1933 in banks with similar health that reopened at different dates. In 1932, on average, banks had more deposits; in 1933, on average, banks had less. We find banks that reopened earlier had more deposits after controlling for this downward trend than banks that reopened later. This is true even when comparing banks that were reopened only several business days apart. Early reopening was a strong signal that overrode other observable information about banks’ strength. In essence, depositors treated all banks that reopened rapidly as if they were healthy banks, regardless of information available about their balance sheets. Banks that reopened later received fewer deposits, but depositors responded more strongly to information about their health. The impact of these signals lasted for nearly a decade, as the differences between early and late reopeners remained through 1940.Building on the bank-level results, we show that deposits increased in counties whose banks on average opened early relative to neighboring counties with similar characteristics whose banks on average reopened later. This analysis confirms a principal fear of policymakers today and in the past: interventions during panics that differentiate banks based on pre-panic behavior and characteristics - particularly those that clearly distinguish between stronger and weaker banks - can redistribute funds through the financial system.Since policymakers today worry that deposit shifts could inhibit economic recovery, the fourth step of our argument, appearing in Section 6, examines whether stigma-induced shifts in deposits across counties inhibited or accelerated economic recovery at the county level. A wide range of empirical and theoretical studies (e.g., King and Levine 1993, Jayaratne and Strahan 1996, Cetorelli and Gambera 2001, Beck and Levine 2004, Beck et al. 2010, Chodorow-Reich 2014, Gilje et al. 2016, Mian et al. 2020, Baron et al. 2021, Cohen et al. 2021) indicate that reducing funds available through financial institutions can reduce economic growth. We find, perhaps surprisingly, no statistically significant relationship between counties where banks reopened early (and whose share of aggregate deposits increased as a result) and the pace of economic recovery. Commerce and industry appear to have recovered on average no quicker in counties whose banks reopened earlier than in comparable counties whose banks reopened later or remained closed. This surprising result has several potential explanations. One is federal government programs that loaned funds directly to businesses. Another is our finding that banks reopening early during the Holiday did not loan more funds than banks that reopened later; the extra deposits that they received, in other words, did not result in extra lending.Our statistical analysis, therefore, yields novel conclusions about the Bank Holiday of 1933 and rescues of banking systems in general. During the Bank Holiday, depositors responded to policymakers’ actions that signaled banks’ health. While we cannot track specific depositors, the stigma of late reopening seems to have shifted financial resources across banks and communities. It had, however, much less of an effect on the rest of the economy. The historical record, in other words, provides little support for policymakers’ justifications for across-the-board interventions during recent financial crises. While targeted treatments of banks in distress did stigmatize institutions revealed to be weaker, these shifts - which have the benefit of limiting moral hazard in the long run - do not appear to have come at a cost of reduced commercial or industrial activity.Our paper sheds light on the literature focused on how financial panics can be ended. Studies have examined tools available for stopping runs (e.g., Calomiris et al. 2004, Cecchetti et al. 2009, Boissay et al. 2020), but as Gorton and Tallman (2018) argue, these tools often cannot quickly change opinions of depositors, especially amidst a crisis. For instance, these actions did not stop runs from occurring during the GFC. Further, such government intervention has been shown to introduce moral hazard (e.g., Acharya and Yorulmazer 2007, Diamond and Rajan 2012). Similar to currency depots in the 1920s (e.g., Carlson et al. 2011), the Bank Holiday was targeted at the confidence of depositors rather than propping up the losses of banks. By mitigating the asymmetric information problem, the Holiday put the nation on solid financial footing allowing funds to flow back to banks without the typical drawbacks of government intervention.Along the same lines, our study of the Bank Holiday relates to policy discussions about Supervisory Capital Assessment Program of 2009 (a.k.a. the GFC stress tests). Geithner (2014, p. 286) states that the stress tests: “aimed to impose transparency on opaque financial institutions and their opaque assets in order to reduce the uncertainty that was driving the panic. It would help markets distinguish between viable banks that were temporarily illiquid and weak banks that were essentially insolvent.” He credits stress tests with reassuring the public and encouraging them to return funds to financial institutions. Subsequent research confirms his claims (e.g., Petrella and Resti 2013) and demonstrates that public release of some information from stress tests could be optimal (e.g., Leitner and Williams 2023). Even then, and the fact that they came 8 months after the collapse of Lehman, the stress tests reports were very cautious to disparage any of the banks or to imply that any were at risk. Our analysis contributes to this stream of research by demonstrating the benefits of the quick release of concise information for all banks, rather than information derived from costly and time-consuming stress tests of large institutions. We also demonstrate that positive responses to reports from stress tests are likely to be a universal phenomenon and not merely a feature of the nineteen financial institutions stress tested in the United States in the spring of 2009.The transparency of the Bank Holiday's reopening decisions provides a new perspective on the stigma literature. Given the explicit avoidance of bank stigma by policymakers, the modern literature has generally focused on facility stigma. For instance, it has been discussed with regard to the discount window (Peristani 1998, Furfine 2001, Afonso et al. 2011, Armantier et al. 2015, Ennis 2019) and the Primary Dealer Credit Facility (Copeland et al. 2014, Krishnamurthy et al. 2014). The smaller bank stigma literature has studied emergency liquidity programs and the scrutiny that banks faced if they accepted emergency assistance (Anbil 2018, Vossmeyer 2019, Anbil and Vossmeyer 2021). Our results show that the public perceived the date of reopening as a signal of bank health. The public treated banks that reopened later like banks that received emergency assistance. The Holiday presents an important difference, however, because the closures were mandatory, removing any selection complications due to a bank's participation decision and allowing us to examine stigma purely from regulators’ signals.The rapid recovery of banks and the aggregate economy immediately following the Bank Holiday corroborates key conclusions of the theoretical literature on the optimal design of government programs for recapitalization and bailouts of banking systems. Philippon and Schnabl (2013) find that efficient recapitalization programs “eliminate both free-riding and opportunistic participation” by banks. Philippon and Wang (2023) find that governments can alleviate the moral hazard induced by ex-post efficient bailouts by liquidating the least healthy banks. Banks’ desire to avoid being the last in the pack induces tournament dynamics that push banks towards the optimal level of risk. The Roosevelt Administration recognized these issues and overcame free-riding, opportunism, and moral hazard by requiring participation by all banks and triaging financial institutions. Illiquid banks reopened. Insolvent banks did not. Compulsory participation also overcame the sorting that occurred during Global Financial Crisis when banks of different types approached emergency lending facilities at different rates (Drechsler et al., 2016).The paper also helps us better understand the path of the Great Depression. Authors have pointed to the role that deposit insurance (e.g., Freidman and Schwartz 1963), changing inflation expectations (e.g., Hausman et al. 2019), and gold restrictions (e.g., Romer 1992) had on the economic rebound, but this study shows that the Bank Holiday helped to put the nation back on a firm financial footing before these events occurred. Based on aggregate deposit information, the reopening of banks was met with public confidence and a wave of deposits back into the system despite many banks remaining closed for months if not permanently. Similar to Pedemonte (2024), who shows that Roosevelt's 1935 fireside chat impacted consumer confidence, the rollout of the Holiday was likely a necessary feature in restoring depositor confidence. Additional actions by Roosevelt, Congress, and the Fed thus might have been more effective at accelerating the country out of the Great Depression because the Bank Holiday had stabilized the financial system.Finally, we provide evidence for the arguments of Park (1991), Conti-Brown and Vanatta (2021), and other scholars (Sargent, 1982; Eggertsson, 2008) who discuss the importance of confidence and expectations in the recovery from the Depression. Our research provides evidence supporting Silber's (2009) conclusions concerning the Bank Holiday. Silber argued the Holiday resuscitated banking and financial markets because the public believed Roosevelt's promise in the Fireside chat that all banks would be safe and because the public understood that the Fed's commitment - in part due to expanded powers the Fed received in the Emergency Banking Act - to provide liquidity to financial institutions established de facto deposit insurance. We provide statistical evidence of Silber's supposition that the Holiday restored depositors’ confidence and that depositors generally accepted Roosevelt's banks-would-be-safe message, but we show that depositors did not believe all of Roosevelt's claims. Depositors did not believe that banks which reopened tomorrow were just as healthy as banks that reopened today. Rather than behaving as if they were agents responding to the introduction of deposit insurance in Diamond and Dybvig (1983) model, as in Silber's analogy, we find that depositors behaved as if they were agents in Martin's (2006) model [which is a mathematical reformulation of Bagehot (1873)], where loans by a lender of last resort eliminate runs on solvent banks while retaining depositors’ desire to entrust their savings to healthy intermediaries.
article.2. Historical background: This section sets out the historical information underlying our analysis. First, it describes the panic of 1933 and the government's response. Second, it discusses the institutional setting and why stigma could have arisen during that crisis. Third, it explores basic patterns in the data.The banking panic in February and March 1933 was the most severe in U.S. history. In a few weeks, the nationwide run drained over a quarter of all deposits from commercial banks (see Fig. 1). The crisis forced banks, savings institutions, investment firms, stock markets, and other financial firms to curtail operations, and it compelled local, state, and the federal government to take aggressive actions, including state-specific banking holidays. The crisis culminated on Friday, March 3, 1933, when the Federal Reserve Bank of New York's reserves fell to their legal limit, and it indicated that it could not open for business the next morning. To forestall the Fed's suspension, Roosevelt declared a nationwide Bank Holiday beginning Monday, March 6.Download: Download high-res image (225KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 1. Weekly Total Deposits as a percent of Deposits on January 4, 1933.Notes: Data from all weekly reporting banks in the United States. Reports from close of business on Wednesdays from January 4 to July 5, 1933. The figure displays total deposits on each date divided by deposits on January 4, 1933. Data obtained from the Board of Governors (1943).The government acted rapidly to reopen the financial system. On March 9, Congress passed the Emergency Banking Act, which empowered the Secretary of the Treasury to issue licenses that allowed banks to reopen and strengthened federal regulation of banks. Reopening decisions were decentralized based on the regulator in charge of the bank along with help from the Treasury and the Reconstruction Finance Corporation (RFC). The Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC) and Federal Reserve were responsible for national banks and state Fed member banks. Each state's banking authority was responsible for their state non-member banks. Regulators received general guidance about how to choose banks to reopen, but no specific instructions. Officials reopened banks that they deemed to be (i) solvent and (ii) sufficiently liquid to satisfy reasonable withdrawal requests. Officials chose based upon their knowledge of each bank's financial status, standards of practice then in use to identify unhealthy banks, and rules of thumb rapidly devised to help them sort through the thousands of decisions that they needed to make to get the financial system up and running in a few days. The initial decisions were based upon existing information from banks’ recent examinations and opinions of the examiners rather than new examinations.The most detailed description of the reopening process comes from Awalt (1969) who was acting Comptroller of the Currency between September 1932 and May 1933. He oversaw the national banking system, witnessed the panic, and was a key director of the government's response. Awalt indicates that work to identify which banks should reopen began on March 8. Authorities understood that speed was important, and decisions would be imperfect. Some weak banks would reopen swiftly, and some strong banks would remain closed for a prolonged period. Jesse Jones, head of the RFC, noted that banks slipped through the cracks: “In those feverish days and nights, it was difficult to decide whether a bank was truly sound…Mistakes were inevitable. A great many unsound banks were allowed to resume business” (Jones and Angly, 1951, p. 21).Some of these “mistakes” were the result of conflicting goals. For instance, Awalt indicated that regulators were concerned about the geographic coverage of reopened banks. The nation needed banks to facilitate payments and prevent business disruptions. “It was necessary that, as far as possible, banks be opened to afford every community with some banking service” (Awalt, 1969, p. 363). One of Awalt's early steps was to plot on a map all the banks that the OCC thought could be reopened without additional liquidity. The initial distribution was too sparse in some areas, which led Awalt to recommend looser criteria in certain regions.Policymakers understood that their actions might signal the health of commercial banks to the public and worried about the consequences of such inferences. On March 12, in his first Fireside Chat, Roosevelt communicated the reopening plan directly to the public and addressed the issue of stigma head on. He explained that most banks would be solvent if the public once again trusted them with their funds. In his view, Americans had been “stampeded by rumors or guesses” and a prolonged reopening process was necessary to “enable the Government to make common sense checkups.” He promised that only sound banks would reopen, and they would be supported by the government. While some banks would open before others, the President emphasized that a “bank that opens on one of the subsequent days is in exactly the same status as the bank that opens tomorrow.” Roosevelt's fireside comments and memoirs of principals such as Awalt (1969) and Jones and Angly (1951) make it clear that regulators were concerned about stigma.Policymakers at the time had a professional understanding of stigma that social scientists have since formalized using game theory models. The theoretical conditions underlying signaling games clearly existed in the 1930s. Some agents (regulators) had more information about bank health than others (depositors and the public). Regulators examined banks periodically and had access to their internal data, audit reports, and senior personnel as well as a vast array of information collected by government agencies and private companies. Federal Reserve officials had information on member banks’ daily reserve balances, discount borrowing, and the quantity and quality of members' collateral presented at the discount window. Regulators also had specialized training and on-the-job experience in how to interpret this information. Alternatively, the public had access to much less information. Data services and government bureaus published bank balance-sheet data at low frequency.Regulators (the informed parties) took an action based on their private information. This action - reopening a bank on a certain date - was readily observable, and its link to private information was public knowledge. So, depositors and the public (the less informed parties) could infer the information that regulators possessed but the public did not. These signals and stigmas could have a lasting effect because the public received little information about banks and had limited ability to update their beliefs. Information revelation, however in this case, could be imperfect since regulators’ actions were not a one-to-one transformation of their private information about bank health. The heads of the OCC and RFC both indicated that some banks opened earlier than warranted by their health to satisfy policy concerns other than financial stability and that “mistakes” were made. Some weak banks reopened early. Some strong banks reopened late. In this case, signals about some banks’ health could be incorrect.In this environment, the government's efforts to reboot the banking system progressed rapidly. Table 1 shows that 81% of Fed member banks and 64% of state non-member banks were allowed to reopen on an unrestricted basis by the end of March. Another 1000 banks were fully licensed to reopen by the end of June. The table also makes clear that there was a middle ground for state banks not mentioned by Roosevelt: restricted reopening. While national banks that had not reopened by March 16 were placed in the hands of conservators, many states passed laws that allowed their state-chartered banks to reopen subject to restrictions on the withdrawal of existing deposits and provision of loans. In most cases, restricted banks could still receive new deposits that were not subject to withdrawal limits. Several states used the approach extensively, while others did not utilize it at all. Table 1 shows that almost 2200 state banks were allowed to reopen under restriction during March, but only about half of all states permitted reopenings with restrictions. When the dust settled, around 4000 banks either closed permanently or had to be substantially recapitalized before reopening.Table 1. Deposits swiftly flowed back into the banking system. Between March 13 and 15, $370 million in gold coin and gold certificates were deposited in the banking system, which was more than all that had been withdrawn during 1933. In the second half of March, another $260 million was returned (Federal Reserve Board, 1934, p. 15). The stock market also soared. The Dow Jones Industrial Average increased 15.3 percent between March 3 (the close of the last trading day before the Bank Holiday) and March 15 (the close of the first day the New York Stock Exchange resumed trading), and nearly doubled by the end of June (Silber, 2009). The deposit rebound continued through the spring, with deposits at weekly-reporting member banks increasing 22% in four months from March 8 to July 5, 1933 (see Fig. 1).Banks that survived the winnowing during the Holiday changed their behavior. The Federal Reserve Board (1933, p. 209) reported that member banks reduced borrowing at the discount window by $1 billion between March 4 and April 5. Bankers acted as if they expected depositors to refrain from running on banks and business prospects to improve.This rebound in deposits was unlikely to have been the result of the creation of federal deposit insurance. The rebound began nine months before federal deposit insurance coverage came into force (on January 1, 1934), three months before the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC) was created by the Banking Act of 1933 (commonly called the Glass-Steagall Act of June 16, 1933), and two months before the proposal to create the FDIC seemed likely to overcome the opposition of large banks, many senators and representatives, and Roosevelt himself. At a press conference on March 8, 1933, when asked about guaranteeing bank deposits, Roosevelt stated “Any form of general guarantee means a definite loss to the Government…We do not wish to make the United States Government liable for the mistakes and errors of individual banks and put a premium on unsound banking in the future.” Roosevelt did not express willingness to compromise on the issue until the end of May, and even then, he insisted on limiting coverage to levels that would primarily cover households and small businesses, but not large firms or wealthy investors.
article.3. Data: Answering the questions asked in this essay - why did regulators reopen some banks rather than others, did regulators’ actions send signals and engender stigmas, and how did depositors and economic growth respond to these choices, signals, and stigmas? - requires quantitative and qualitative information. The quantitative information consists of data on all the commercial banks in operation, the dates and condition of each bank's reopening, and information about shocks impacting banks during the contraction of the early 1930s. We collected, compiled, and digitized this information for our project. The qualitative information consists of accounts indicating the data that regulators had at their disposal, the ways in which they used this information to inform their decisions, the information that regulators did not have, and procedures that regulators might have used but which sources indicate they did not employ.Our quantitative data come from a range of sources. The identities, characteristics, and balance sheets for all U.S. commercial banks at the June call in 1929, 1932, and 1933 come from Rand McNally's Bankers Directory published in July of each year.5 This directory was the most widely used compendium of bank balance sheets available to the public during the 1930s.6 The data contain broad balance sheet categories (e.g., total loans, total deposits, etc.), but do not contain information such as underperforming loans or management quality used in modern studies. As discussed in Appendix Section D, even the Federal Reserve's call reports which contained more granular balance sheet categories did not contain information on management or losses. Moreover, as we show in the Appendix Section A, we find similar results when including additional balance sheet risk variables that were not included in Rand McNally. Given the nature of balance sheets, it is not surprising that most risk measures are correlated.To form our sample, we drop out branches, private banks, cash depositories, and mutual savings banks using information in Rand McNally. We determine which state banks were Fed members at the end of 1932 and their Federal Reserve District using the Annual Report of the Federal Reserve Board for 1932.We extend the Rand McNally balance sheet data forward in two different ways. First, we collect annual national bank balance sheet data for 1928 through 1940 from the OCC to provide a longer-run lens to examine these issues. Second, we obtain county-level deposit aggregates that were published in county databooks from 1941 through 1964 and collected by Paul Rhode. The data allow us to show how long the deposit differences across locations persisted.To determine which banks were in operation at the start of the Bank Holiday, we extract all banks that ceased operations or merged with other institutions before March 5, 1933 using the Federal Reserve's Division of Bank Operations St. 6386 forms. The forms list changes in status of each commercial bank between January 1929 and March 1933 (Richardson, 2007). We cross match this information on changes in bank status reported in Rand McNally in July 1933.We determine the fate of each bank between the onset of the Holiday, March 6, and June 1933 using internal Fed documents that list all banks that were fully reopened or reopened with restrictions in each district by week. Comprehensive weekly reports exist up until March 29.Our efforts indicate that 16,790 commercial banks were in business on March 6. At the end of March, 11,793 banks were reported as being fully reopened. Another 1685 banks were reopened under restrictions. Fig. 2 shows the distribution of reopenings by county. In many states, reopening rates in the weeks immediately after the Holiday varied substantially across adjacent jurisdictions. In some counties, a high fraction (even 100%) of banks reopened immediately; while in neighboring counties, a much lower fraction (often below 60%) reopened.Download: Download high-res image (1MB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 2. Fraction of Commercial Banks Reopened in March 1933 by County.We supplement the bank data with a wide range of supporting variables. Demographic and economic information on the county in which the bank was located comes from the census of 1929 from Haines (2010) and Haines et al. (2018). County-level electoral data come from Clubb et al. (2006). We also collect information on county-level economic activity over the period, including the value of retail sales from Fishback et al. (2005)7, the number of individuals filing tax returns from Fishback et al. (2011), the value of wholesale sales from Bureau of the Census (1937a), and the value added by manufacturing from Bureau of the Census (1937b).Table 2 presents summary statistics for banks grouped by their reopening date. The top part of the table provides averages before the Holiday and the bottom part shows how deposits changed after the Holiday for the sample of surviving banks. There are a few notable takeaways from the table. First, the earliest reopeners are generally larger and more likely to be members of the Federal Reserve System, which aligns with the historical record. Second, in the weeks following the initial reopening, there does not appear to be a specific rank or ordering in balance sheet characteristics. article.4. Statistical evidence of signals: The initial step of our analysis examines key predictions of signaling models. We ask why regulators reopened some banks rapidly and not others. This step is necessary to determine what information the public might have gleaned from the rate of reopening. We will show that the reopening was strongly correlated with balance sheet measures about banks’ health in the hands of regulators but the signals also contained noise, since regulators reopened some unhealthy banks rapidly while some healthy banks remained closed. The noise arose because regulators had limited time and information to determine which banks should reopen and may have been influenced by political factors and policy objectives other than bank health.To uncover common patterns underlying regulators’ decisions, we employ an iterative statistical approach. Initially, we examine individual factors related to the bank itself. These include measures of potential risk based on balance sheet position in 1932: the logarithm of total assets, loans to assets, paid-in capital, surplus, and undivided profits to assets (i.e., capital to assets), and cash, exchanges, and due from banks to total deposits (i.e., cash to deposits). These measures are similar to those found to be correlated with the probability of bank failure in academic papers examining similar eras such as the 1920s agricultural depression (Jaremski and Wheelock, 2020), and Great Depression (White, 1984; Richardson and Troost, 2009). They are also similar to measures that regulators use to determine the health of bank balance sheets during modern stress tests. We also calculate the changes in the logarithms of deposits and loans from 1929 to 1932 to capture the pre-trends of banks going into the Holiday. Depositors and regulators were certainly able to see bank runs and loan losses across the broader span of time. Finally, we include the logarithm of the bank's age as well as indicators for whether the bank was a national bank or a state Fed member bank (with state non-member banks as the excluded group) and whether it operated any branches outside of the city of its headquarters.Second, we include basic demographic control variables on the county itself. These variables include: the logarithm of population in 1929, fraction of the county living in an urban location of 2500 or more people in 1929, the fraction of the adult population that is illiterate in 1929, the fraction of the population that is non-white in 1929, the fraction of owner-operator farms that had mortgage debt in 1929, indicators for whether the bank was located in a city that was designated a Central Reserve City, Reserve City or had a clearinghouse in 1932, and the logarithm of the number of banks in the county in 1932. The variables account for regional variation that could influence bank operations but were unlikely to influence regulators directly. We, therefore, include them in the models but do not report their statistics to save space. We also include information on economic and political factors of each county. These include: the logarithm of the number of farms per capita, the logarithm of the number of manufacturing establishments per capita, and the fractions of votes for the Democrat candidates in the House of Representatives elections of 1930 and the Presidential election of 1932.Third, we add information on the rest of the banking community within the bank's town. As highlighted by Awalt, geographic proximity was important to keep the payments system afloat. A bank's relative position rather than the exact value of its position thus might have been important when there were few options available. In addition to a dummy variable for whether the bank was the only one in the town, we control for the fraction of those other banks that were national banks, the fraction of those other banks that were state member banks, the logarithm of average assets in other banks, the average ratios of loans to assets, capital to assets, and cash to deposits in other banks in the town. For brevity, we suppress several of these variables from our output tables but they are available upon request.Fourth, we control for the state in which the bank was located. The narrative evidence indicated that various regulators had different risk preferences and Mitchener (2005, 2007) demonstrates the importance of different regulatory regimes in each state. Fig. 2 has also shown that certain geographic areas had a much lower proportion of reopened banks. We include state-fixed effects to strip out any differences across states and focus only on variation across banks within the same state.9We model the probability of fully reopening anytime during March using a logit framework and estimate the model by maximum likelihood:(1)ReopenedMarchi=1{α+BalSheeti′βBS+Countyi′βC+EcPoli′βEP+OtherBanksi′βOB+Statei′βS+εi>0}where ReopenedMarchi is an indicator for whether bank i was fully reopened by March 29, 1933, BalSheeti is a vector of balance sheet measures of bank i in June 1932 and the changes in the logarithms of loans and deposits between June 1929 and June 1932, Countyi is a vector of county demographic and locational controls for bank i, EcPoli is a vector of county economic and political variables for bank i, OtherBanksi is a vector of information on other banks in the same town as bank i, Statei is a vector of state fixed effects, and εi is the error term which is clustered by county. All models also include the logarithm of bank age.The marginal effects of the estimated coefficients for Eq. (1) are presented in Table 3. Because about 460 banks in 1932 were not present in 1929, we start with a specification without the changes in loans and deposits 1929 to 1932 to show that the results are similar with and without these banks. In the first two columns, reopenings in March are positively associated with the size of the bank's assets, the amount of capital to assets, the amount of cash to deposits, and the change in deposits between 1929 and 1932, whereas they are negatively associated with the change in loans between 1929 and 1932. Moreover, the estimates suggest that those banks that were subject to higher requirements and supervision (i.e., national banks and state Fed member banks) were more likely to be reopened in March than state non-member banks. The bank-level results remain even when county-level controls and state-fixed effects are added.Table 3. Several of the variables played a statistically significant role in reopening decisions. First, banks in counties with more farms had a higher probability of being reopened. This matches with the period as farmers in most of the county relied on state bank credit to finance the crop and purchase land (see, for instance, Jaremski and Fishback 2018). Second, banks in areas that seemed to swing against their 1930 pattern and vote for Roosevelt in 1932 had fewer reopenings than other locations. It is possible that this was an attempt to win the future political votes of those places that were not already solidly in Roosevelt's corner.10 The inclusion of state-fixed effects in column (5) reduces the statistical significance of manufacturing establishments and Democrat voting in 1930 but does not eliminate the effect of the other variables.In column (4), we add the town-level bank comparison values. Here, the only variable that is consistently significant is fraction of banks in the town that are national banks. The positive coefficient suggests that there might be some positive spillover effects of good banking areas, rather than a relative competition amongst banks in a location.Even though the balance sheet risk factors and other variables are relevant predictors of reopening decisions, it is important to point out that even the fully specified model with state-fixed effects only correctly classifies 77.7% of reopening decisions (in-sample classification). To assess out-of-sample accuracy and to explore important features in model specification, we consider several binary classification machine learning methods, including random forest, support vector machines, k-nearest neighbor, neural networks, and logistic regression. For each approach, we use 50% of our sample for training and 50% for testing, and include all variables except for the state indicators. Further, we run each model 20 times, randomizing the training sample, to ensure each bank receives a prediction. The most important features from the random forest specification - the most accurate model by a slight margin - for the probability of reopening are: Cash to Deposits, Change in Ln(Deposits) 1929–1932, and Capital to Assets. Of the 31 variables in the random forest model, these three variables carry 23% of the weight, 10.5%, 6.5%, and 6.0%, respectively.11 Combining the predictions across the 100 estimations and using a 0.50 predictive probability cutoff for the 0 and 1 predictions, the overall accuracy is about 75.3% (the unconditional probability of reopening is 69%). Thus, the information from the combined model predictions is useful, but the signal was imperfect.In Fig. 3, we provide a histogram of the average predicted probability of reopening from the binary classification machine learning methods separated by whether the predictions were accurate or not. The figure shows hundreds of banks with predicted probabilities of reopening greater than 85% that did not reopen and hundreds of banks with predicted probabilities less than 40% that reopened. Despite balance sheet factors playing an important role in our models, it is clear that the signal of reopening revealed more information than just bank health. Some misclassification is from unobservables, such as management quality. For instance, the models predict that Harriman National Bank and Trust Co in New York City should have reopened early, as it was large and liquid in the 1932 call reports. However, the bank was not reopened in March 1933, and in 1934, the bank's president was convicted of fraud. Other misclassification is likely due to regulators guessing asset values. Jones stated, “The plunge in values, particularly market ones, made one man's guess as good, or bad, as another's in assessing the probable worth of many a bank's portfolio” (Jones and Angly 1951, p. 21).Download: Download high-res image (209KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 3. The black represents observations that were correctly classified and the gray represents observations that were misclassified.Appendix A1 on Robustness addresses several threats to inference including regulators’ incentives and limited information available to depositors. Foremost amongst these is the potential for missing information. If unobservable characteristics like management quality or banking services determined the fate of banks, this information's exclusion from our regressions might bias our estimates. To address these issues, we re-estimate the model with samples separated by the number of banks in a city. Since depositors’ knowledge of the characteristics of each bank in their town diminished and the probability of having additional services offered increased as the number of banks in their town grew, the coefficients should differ across regressions if these factors influenced reopening. We find no significant differences indicating that the most prominent sources of unobservable characteristics likely do not distort our results.We also show that the imperfect signal is not driven by observable risk factors that are contained in other data. Specifically, the OCC provides information on bills payable (i.e., costly funding) and separately reports US Treasury bonds from other securities. Both an indicator for whether the bank had any bills payable and its ratio of US Treasury bonds to total securities significantly predict reopenings but do not dramatically change the effect of other factors or the classification rate. In this way, we are confident that despite the limitations of the Rand McNally data that the results are not being driven by observable risk factors.Appendix A1 also includes an additional series of robustness checks on the reopening regressions. We re-estimate the regressions controlling for the changes in county-level economic conditions during the Great Depression using the logarithm change in retail sales per capita from 1929 to 1933. We re-estimate the regressions when dropping out any county with a city of 25,000 or more people as large cities had many more banks competing and more diverse business interests. We also re-estimate the regressions only for those state non-member banks that either reopened fully or had a restricted reopening in March, as well as break up the sample by bank type: national banks, state Fed member banks, and state non-member bank to capture the different regulatory agencies. In all cases, the predictive power of the balance sheet factors is similar to Table 3, which alleviates concerns about the potential confounders.
article.5. Signals, stigmas, and depositors’ rea: The previous section shows that reopening dates should have sent noisy signals about banks’ health. On average, healthier banks opened earlier, but many weak banks were also opened early, while many healthy banks were opened late. This section tests whether the public responded to these noisy signals at the bank and county-level.5.1. Reopening dates and deposits at individual banksWe begin by analyzing deposits for all banks from Rand McNally for 1932 and 1933. Because banks that remained closed in July 1933 (many of which were never reopened) did not publish balance sheet information in Rand McNally, we have a balanced sample of surviving banks that were fully reopened by July 1933. We exploit changes in deposits at banks reopened on different dates while controlling for bank health. We identify the consequences of late-reopening stigma, in other words, by comparing the change in deposits at banks that reopened earlier with banks of the same health that reopened later. The linear model estimated by OLS is:(2)ΔDepositsi=a+ReopenedMarchi′βR+BalSheeti′βBS+Countyi′βC+EcPoli′βEP+Statei′βS+εiwhere ΔDepositsi is the change in the logarithm of deposits for bank i between the June call dates in 1932 and 1933, and the rest of the variables retain their previous definitions. Because the sample is now restricted to surviving banks, the results for ReopenedMarchi are for banks that reopened earlier, relative to banks that reopened later.The inclusion of a bank's fundamentals is important for separating depositors’ responses to the signal of reopening relative to typical monitoring behavior. As shown in other papers (e.g., Gorton and Pennacchi 1990, Calomiris and Wilson 2004, Calomiris and Jaremski 2019, Anderson et al. 2023), informed depositors are sensitive to observable bank risk and respond positively to the same factors that caused regulators to quickly reopen banks. While we have shown that many banks with shaky fundamentals were reopened, regulators reopened many banks based on their health. By controlling for each bank's balance sheet, we can illustrate the relative power of the signal sent by regulators compared to traditional bank fundamentals.The first two columns of Table 4 present coefficients estimated for Eq. (2) for all banks with and without the balance sheet factors. The data show that rapidly reopened banks saw a significantly higher change in deposits, relative to banks that fully reopened between April and July 1933. Even after controlling for a bank's fundamentals in 1932 and the changes in deposits and loans from 1929 to 1932, a bank that fully reopened in March rather than later had deposit changes 17.9% higher in July 1933. This pattern is a standard symptom of bank stigma, which suggests individuals put fewer deposits in these banks because they believed they were weaker than their fundamentals would suggest or were in some other way worse than banks reopened earlier.Table 4. First, we re-estimate the model with samples separated by the number of banks in a city and bank types. As previously described, banks in larger cities were more likely to offer additional bank services that could be attractive to depositors in the rebound. Similarly, national banks operated under a consistent set of tight regulation across the country and thus there are fewer unobservable factors within the group. Despite the limitations of the Rand McNally data, it does not seem like the results are driven by risk factors that were visible for some banks but not others.5.2. Reopening dates and deposits at individual banks–surrounding depositsThe evidence presented in the previous section suggests that those banks that were chosen to reopen in March had higher changes in deposits over time. A reasonable question is where those deposits came from. This section attempts to shed light on this question through additional narrative and empirical evidence.The deposit inflow was likely money returning to the system. If a depositor's original bank remained closed, then they would have been unable to withdraw their deposits in order to place them with another bank. So, the initial balance sheet change observed is more likely due to “mattress” money returning to banks, as opposed to resources shifting away from a bank that had not reopened yet. This agrees with Silber (2009) who argues that the de facto government safety net for reopened banks brought money back to the system, and the Federal Reserve Board (1934) that argues that the return of money was from hoarders rather than active circulation. Moreover, the differential change is not due to the costs of moving deposits back to the original bank after it reopened. Because they could not withdraw money from their original account, depositors would have had to keep their original deposit account at the non-reopened bank which would have eliminated the costs of moving money back to their originally preferred bank once it reopened. As we show later, quickly reopened banks maintained their extra deposits for over a decade, suggesting choices made during the Bank Holiday shifted depositor preferences in the long-term.Comparing banks that reopened with and without restrictions reinforces this point. In columns (5) and (6) of Table 4, we restrict the sample to only state non-member banks and examine the difference between (i) banks that fully reopened in March, (ii) banks that had restricted reopenings in March but fully reopened by July, and (iii) banks that did not reopen in March but fully reopened between April and July. State non-member banks that immediately and unconditionally reopened had larger changes in deposits (by 20.3%) when compared to other state banks. Banks that reopened under restrictions in March 1933 saw higher changes in deposits (by 8.0%) than those that were not opened in any capacity in March 1933, but instead reopened in April, May, or June. These results show the inflow of funds to fully reopened banks was not solely due to them being the only banks accepting funds. If that was the case, then there would be no difference between restricted and unrestricted banks. The inflow of funds was also not due to the disruption of customer relationships because they remained in place. When banks reopened, in other words, they had the same depositors with the same deposits and the same borrowers with the same loan contracts as on the day they closed. The inflow of funds was also not due to early reopened banks offering services to depositors that they did not receive at banks operating under restrictions. Restrictions only limited withdrawal of deposits in a bank before it reopened. All deposits received by banks after reopening could be used without restrictions. Services offered to new or deposited funds, in other words, did not differ between banks that reopened with restrictions and banks that did not. These facts indicate that the large difference in deposit flows between restricted and unrestricted banks (as well as the large difference in deposit flows between banks reopening a few days apart) was the signal sent by early reopening.While the inflow of deposits to early reopeners is not driven by depositors moving their funds from a closed bank, it could be driven by deposits that would normally go to another bank in a town but could not because that bank had remained closed. If quickly reopened banks were more often located near these remaining closed banks, then the difference in deposits might be a function of forced deposit flows (particularly in the first few months). We test this hypothesis by creating an indicator for whether a reopened bank was in the same city as a bank that had not yet reopened in July 1933. About 11.5% of banks reopened in March 1933 were in a location that had a bank that remained closed through July 1933, whereas 12.0% of banks reopened between April and June 1933 were in a location that had one.To consider the closed bank hypothesis in an econometrics framework, we adapted Eq. (2) to include an indicator for whether or not at least one nearby bank remained closed after July 1933 and an interaction of that variable with our early reopening indicator. The results in Table 6 show that deposits at reopened banks did rise by more in areas with closed banks. However, the effect is relatively small compared to the effect of early reopening. Additionally, the interaction is insignificant, meaning that a nearby bank closure does not modify the early reopening effect on deposit changes. The evidence suggests that the stigma effect we are measuring is not necessarily driven by differential closure rates around quickly reopened banks.Table 6. Reopening dates and long run deposits at individual banksThe evidence from the Rand McNally data is consistent with the hypothesis that depositors favored banks that reopened early and discriminated against banks that reopened later. However, the limited number of time periods prevents us from testing whether this differential behavior continued further in time. The OCC data are observed at the end of each year, providing balance sheet observations closer to the start of the Holiday.We estimate a bank fixed-effect panel data model with interactions between the reopening variable and time-fixed effects. The time interactions before the Holiday provide a relative measure as to how banks that would be reopened in March 1933 were trending during the Great Depression downturn, while the interactions after 1932 show how those banks changed after the Bank Holiday. However, there is a significant difference between the size of that relative change in deposits for the quickest reopening versus others. Specifically, those that were opened within the first few days after the Holiday saw a much higher change in deposits than those opened in the second week, and those opened in the second week saw slightly higher change than those opened in the third week. Over time, the deposit differences between banks reopened in the second and third week narrow, but the difference for banks reopened in the first week remains. Regulators could not feasibly review and reopen all sound banks in the first week. Nevertheless, those first, second, and third week decisions had lasting effects.Taken as a whole, depositors preferred banks that were reopened quickly even though many other banks opened just a few days or weeks later. Depositors thus seem to have taken the speed of reopening as a cleaner signal of financial stability than traditional observable fundamentals, stigmatizing slowly reopened banks. The persistent effect lends weight to the hypothesis that the Bank Holiday's release of information from the timing of reopenings was responsible for the public's increased confidence rather than Roosevelt's promise to support reopened banks. If the unconditional guarantee of support was fully responsible for the public response, then we would expect a fast convergence of balance sheets.5.4. County-level deposit differencesOur findings regarding individual banks should be assessed in light of Fig. 2, which shows that reopening rates varied substantially across counties. While some of the cross-county variation reflected differences in bank health, much arose from idiosyncratic delays in the reopening process and non-economic factors like politics. This section asks whether inter-county variation in reopening rates shifted deposits across counties in the short and long term. Identification is based on differences in the fraction of quickly reopened banks and thus mixes counties whose banks reopened later in 1933 with the many that never reopened. The analysis captures the overall effect of regulators’ decisions on which banks to reopen.We consider the log change in deposits at the county-level from July 1932 (only considering banks that survived to the Bank Holiday) through July 1933, aggregating Rand McNally data to the county level. Further, we employ the county-level deposit aggregates from 1941 through 1964 that were published in county databooks and collected by Paul Rhode to extend the analysis forward in time. While it is plausible for individual depositors to shift funds among banks in the same town and maybe to banks in adjacent towns, neighboring counties were farther away. Travelling to them was costly in terms of time and money. These costs probably deterred individual depositors from opening accounts or doing substantial banking business in banks in neighboring counties. Individual depositors in counties where banks reopened late on average may, however, have substituted savings away from commercial banks and into alternative depositories, like savings banks and postal savings, or alternative investments, like equity-accumulating life insurance, savings bonds, or cash held at home. These shifts in portfolio composition could account for the patterns that we observe.Business deposits were more mobile than individual deposits. Over 100,000 businesses in the United States had operations in multiple locations, often across counties. These included almost all the firms in the United States that were net creditors to banks (i.e. whose deposits exceeded loans from banks). These organizations often possessed accounts in banks in multiple counties, did large volumes of transactions via check, and could easily have shifted funds from banks in one location to another (Del Angel et al., 2024). Another reason business deposits could shift location was the rise in medium term bank loans and expansion of trade credit documented by Jacoby and Saulnier (1942).5.5. However, this begs the question of whether those banks were observably safer going forward as well. If quickly reopened banks maintained the appearance of safety relative to others, then depositors’ decisions might have been about future fundamentals rather than past ones. The coefficients and standard errors are provided in Appendix A2.Taken as a whole, the balance sheet regressions indicate that depositors took the speed of reopening as a signal of financial stability, stigmatizing slowly reopened banks. Depositors seemingly preferred those banks that were reopened quickly even though many other banks opened just a few weeks later, and changes in the fundamentals at quickly reopened banks did not deter them to move their funds to other banks.Moreover, the evidence suggests that this shift of deposits across banks could have ambiguous effects on economic growth. Specifically, quickly reopened banks did not maintain their loan to asset ratios as deposits rose. Instead, they expanded their holdings of bonds and securities, rather than local lending. In this way, it does not seem like quickly reopened banks successfully took over lending relationships from banks that either were slow to reopen or never reopened at all. As discussed by Bernanke (1983) and many others, bank lending relationships are hard to transfer and take time to develop with new banks.5.6. Ruling out alternative explanationsWhile the results of the analysis are consistent with our claims concerning stigma, the extant data and existing statistical methods cannot rule out all potential alternative hypotheses. The most salient threats to inference involve regulators conditioning reopening on information that (i) they observed, (ii) was correlated with changes in deposits from June 1932 to June 1933 (i.e. our dependent variable), and (iii) we do not observe and thus cannot control for. To exclude these threats (or at least limit their plausibility), we bring to bear additional historical evidence.The initial threat to consider is the possibility that regulators’ reopening decisions depended upon the deposits that each bank lost during the panic. Routine procedures would not have generated this data, but regulators could have collected this one piece of information on an emergency basis. Regulators might have initially reopened banks that lost the most deposits (but did not fail). This policy would seem sensible. Regulators were trying to get deposits to flow back into the system. Regulators wanted to reopen healthy banks. Banks that paid out a large fraction of their deposits in a short period of time without suspending operations or failing outright demonstrated that they were liquid and solvent. Regulators clearly knew the quality of their assets because those assets served as collateral for loan from Federal Reserve banks, and the Reserve Bank discount officers had appraised these assets and had them in their possession. If regulators reopened these banks first, then our estimates would understate stigma, since earlier reopeners would have had a more rapid rebound than we estimate.18 Regulators might also have done the opposite, and reopened banks with larger deposit losses later. In this case, our estimates would be biased away from zero, and our estimates would overstate stigma. If regulators used one approach in some locales and the other approach in other locales, our estimates might or might not be biased, but the standard errors of our estimates would almost certainly be.While it was possible for regulators to have collected this information in the early days of the Holiday, we do not believe that they did so. We consulted regulatory reports, memoirs, newspapers, and the archives of the OCC and the Fed. We list these sources in the data section and describe the most important in additional detail in Appendix Section D. None mention efforts to collect this information or report that this information was collected. None describe regulators using this information when making decisions. Instead, the sources do mention the suspension of routine data collection during the Holiday.19 The sources also mention regulators being overburdened with work. Many had to deal with the hundreds of banks that suspended operations before the government shuttered the banking system. All those banks had to be evaluated to determine if they could reopen or should enter liquidation. Many examiners were assigned to help with discount lending, which expanded 100-fold during the three-week panic.20 Historical sources mention all these activities, but they do not mention an effort to collect or use data on deposit losses during the panic, which we believe they would have, if such as effort occurred. The archives of the OCC, RFC, and the Fed also contain no information about deposit losses during the panic, although they contain extensive data on bank outcomes during the panic, including detailed lists of the banks reopening each week. Since we see no evidence that regulators possessed detailed information on deposit losses during the panic, we think this threat to inference is possible, but unlikely.Certainly, Fed officials would have had more information on Fed member balances and borrowing as well as more detailed information for weekly reporting banks in financial centers. Looking at Appendix Table A4, we find that the effect of early reopening on member banks was larger than that of non-member banks (28.3 percent vs. 16.6 percent), but the effect on non-member banks was still quite sizable. These differences suggest that it is plausible that reopening decisions may have incorporated unobservable information about deposit outflows. But our baseline result in Table 4 (17.9 percent) is quite a bit closer to the effect for nonmember banks who make up the majority of the system.Another change on the liability side of banks’ balance sheets that could also have generated the appearance of stigma is recapitalization. Perhaps banks that reopened early received injections of capital from federal agencies, state authorities, or the market, which encouraged increases in deposits. The federal government did purchase preferred stock in thousands of banks, but majority of these purchases occurred after the incorporation of the FDIC in the summer of 1933 and most occurred after December 1933. This recapitalization program, in other words, was implemented almost entirely after our analysis concludes. State authorities never purchased equity in commercial banks; their budgets were tight and they asked federal agencies to take the lead on this issue. The market also does not appear to have recapitalized commercial banks. Increases in banks’ paid-up capital from June 1932 to June 1933 were rare and uncorrelated with reopening dates. Moreover, no statistical differences in capital existed between banks that reopened early and late (see Appendix A2 Table A8).Another possibility on the liability side of banks’ balance sheets that could have generated the appearance of stigma is changes in banks’ deposit services. One possibility is banks that reopened earlier improved their deposit services or offered some advantageous services to depositors who switched from other banks. During the Depression, some commercial banks attracted depositors from mutual savings banks by purchasing their mutual-savings shares. Newspapers describe this activity and published tables of the prices (or markdowns) that banks would pay for shares from different mutual-savings banks. Newspapers also reveal that regulators and clearing houses expedited access to deposits at failed commercial banks by assigning nearby commercial banks as agents that would provide cash or deposit services to individuals awaiting payment of frozen funds.21 We find, however, no evidence that commercial banks that opened earlier advanced cash against deposits in banks that reopened later, or that a market for late-reopening deposits existed, or that regulators in any way encouraged the transition of deposits or depositors towards banks that reopened early. Moreover, our results show no significant difference between the reopening estimate for large financial centers where such services might have emerged and rural areas where such services would not have.Another possibility is banks that reopened later only reopened after forcing depositors to accept write offs on their deposits. The Fed's archives show that a handful of banks reopened in 1932 after depositors and stockholders agreed to symmetrically reduce their claims against the bank so that the institution could reopen. The RFC also reports a small number of write-offs during recapitalizations and reorganizations that helped banks reopen in late 1933. We see no reports of write offs, however, in March and April of 1933, when all reports indicate that all banks reopened with the same balance sheets, liabilities, and assets that they had when the Holiday began. Our regression results hold if we limit the analysis to this period when historical sources indicate that no write offs occurred.A final question related to the liability side of the balance sheet involves banks’ deposit franchise value. Drechsler et al. (2023) describe this arrangement for modern banks, where increases (decreases) in market interest rates relative to the rates banks pay to depositors increase (decrease) the spread banks earn on deposits, a risk which banks hedge with long-term investments whose value fluctuates inversely with market interest rates. This dynamic did not come into play during the Banking Holiday. Before that event, almost all banks’ investments matured in the short term, typically a few months or less. From the start of the panic in January 1933 to the end of our study in June 1933, deposit rates, loan rates, Fed policy rates, other market interest rates, and policy rates moved little and essentially 1 for 1. So, banks’ franchise value should have changed little during the period of our study. Changes in banks’ deposit franchise value, in other words, could not explain the correlation between reopening date and deposit rebound during the Banking Holiday. We cannot rule out, however, the potential that changes in deposit franchise value contributed to stigma's persistence, since amendments to the Federal Reserve Act compelled the central bank to cap deposit interest rates. The cap for demand deposits became binding in the later 1930s, about the same time that banks began extending substantial quantities of medium-term loans and that banks began purchasing substantial quantities of long-term government bonds (Jacoby and Saulnier, 1942).We can also rule out a range of alternatives on the asset side of banks’ balance sheets. A principal concern would be that regulators decided reopening dates based upon updated information about the value of banks’ assets, or prospects for banks’ lending growth, or prospects for economic recovery in the regions around each bank. Historical sources indicate that regulators did not update information about banks’ portfolios while making decisions. While the Treasury's instructions about how to determine when to reopen banks were vague, they clearly stated that they should be based upon existing information and did not request the collection of new information. Even if they had, collecting information about asset quality - unlike information about deposit losses - would have been costly and time-consuming.22 Again, as Jesse Jones stated, “The plunge in values, particularly market ones, made one man's guess as good, or bad, as another's in assessing the probable worth of many a bank's portfolio” (Jones and Angly, 1951, p. 21). That is why examiners scrutinized only portions of each bank's past-due loans during examinations, and examiners conducted these examinations infrequently, usually once or twice each year.We believe the other possible alternatives were unlikely for similar reasons. Examiners lacked objective information on prospects for banks’ loan growth or counties economic recovery. Neither the data nor methods to make the projections existed. In addition, our analyses in the previous and following sections demonstrate that reopening dates were uncorrelated with loan expansion and economic growth. So, conditioning reopening dates on accurate projections of loan and economic growth could not have generated the correlations in our paper, even if examiners had been able to observe it.
article.6. Consequences of stigma for commerce a: The previous section demonstrates that depositors read early reopening as a signal of bank quality, stigmatizing banks that reopened later. This result raises the specter of policymaker's fears, that the signals and stigmas sent by procedures for rebooting the banking system reallocated resources and impacted the economic recovery. Their fears seem plausible given the wealth of empirical studies on the positive effects of banks on economic growth in both historical (e.g., Jaremski 2014, Rajan and Ramcharan 2015, 2016, Carlson et al., 2022) and modern periods (e.g., King and Levine 1993, Jayaratne and Strahan 1996, Cetorelli and Gambera 2001, Beck and Levine 2004, Chodorow-Reich 2014, Gilje et al. 2016). Additionally, evidence demonstrates that economic activity contracted more in counties that experienced more bank failures and larger deposit drains during the contraction from 1929 to 1933 (e.g., Bernanke 1983, Richardson and Troost 2009, Ziebarth 2013, Mladjan 2019, Cohen et al. 2021). Theoretical models describe that the availability of banks (and confidence in them) allow money to be drawn out of mattresses and loaned out to the betterment of the local community (Diamond and Dybvig, 1983). Without banks or trust in the system, deposits would be held by individuals and loans would be limited. In this section, we examine how the differential treatment of banks during the Holiday impacted the economic recovery of communities in which those banks operated.We examine the Holiday's effect on a range of county-level economic outcomes. First, retail sales captures 70% of gross domestic output. Second, the number of individuals filing federal income tax returns captures the number of individuals whose incomes exceeded the threshold for filing. Third, wholesale sales captures county-level production and commerce. Finally, the value added by manufacturing captures the increase in gross domestic product in a county due to activities of manufacturing firms. We employ a cross-sectional linear model where the outcomes are the logarithm change in each variable from 1933 through 1935 and logarithm change in retail sales and tax returns for 1933 through 1939 (data from 1939 is extant only for those two variables). We thus capture different types of medium and long-run growth that the presence or absence of banking might influence. The models also control for the change in each outcome variable from 1929 to 1933 and the change the number of banks from 1929 to the start of the Bank Holiday to account for the depth of the Depression.
